chunk_summary: This section discusses criteria for escalating to oral nutritional supplements (ONS) or enteral tube feeding in children with inadequate oral intake or unsafe feeding, emphasizing monitoring, caregiver involvement, and adherence to WHO guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand when to escalate from oral feeding to ONS or enteral tube feeding.
Learn the criteria for initiating nasogastric or gastrostomy feeding in children.
Recognize the importance of monitoring and reassessment during nutritional interventions.
Identify risks such as refeeding syndrome and strategies to preserve feeding skills.
key_facts: ONS should be used as a time-limited bridge and reassessed every 2–4 weeks.
Enteral tube feeding is indicated for unsafe swallowing, severe wasting (SAM), or prolonged feeding times with fatigue.
Initial enteral feeding options include nasogastric (short-to-medium term) and gastrostomy (long-term).
Hospitalization is required for medical instability, dehydration, edema, or suspected sepsis.
WHO guidelines recommend monitoring for refeeding syndrome and ensuring micronutrient supplementation in severely malnourished children.
reference_quote: Monitor for refeeding risks in severely malnourished children as per WHO guidance, ensure micronutrient supplementation, and maintain oral stimulation and safe tasting opportunities to preserve feeding skills while tube feeding is used.
document_key_topics: Criteria for escalation to ONS or enteral feeding
Preservation of feeding skills during tube feeding
Monitoring and reassessment in nutritional interventions
